symbols: stigmata on hand — bearing the wounds of Christ
book — the Gospel and Franciscan Rule
grey habit with cord belt — Franciscan poverty
description: A full-length panel painting of St. Francis of Assisi standing in a pointed Gothic arch against a gold background. He wears a grey Franciscan habit tied with a knotted cord belt and sandals. He holds a red-bound book in one hand and raises the other, which shows the stigmata (the wound of Christ's nail). His face is lean and ascetic with a short beard. A golden halo surrounds his tonsured head. The style is characteristic of 14th-century Italian Gothic panel painting with its elegant simplicity and gold leaf background.
theological_description: St. Francis of Assisi (1181/82-1226) is depicted with his two primary attributes: the stigmata and the book (representing both the Gospel and the Franciscan Rule). Francis received the stigmata — the five wounds of Christ's crucifixion — on Mount La Verna in 1224, the first recorded case in Christian history. This miraculous event visually confirmed Francis' radical identification with Christ crucified, fulfilling Paul's words: 'I bear on my body the marks of Jesus' (Galatians 6:17). Francis' life of radical poverty, based on a literal reading of Christ's instructions to the apostles (Matthew 10:7-10), initiated a spiritual revolution that renewed the medieval Church.

scripture_refs.reason: I bear on my body the marks of Jesus — the stigmata visible on Francis' hand
Go, preach the kingdom, take no possessions — Francis' foundational text
I have been crucified with Christ — Francis' total identification with the crucified Lord
